key_insight: A frontier language model, constrained to reason over explicit reaction networks, can generate experimentally testable catalyst-selectivity hypotheses and uncover actionable levers for CO2 electroreduction.
engineering_relevance: This enables mechanism-guided catalyst design that moves beyond static descriptors toward targeted control of selectivity in complex electrochemical processes.
method: Built a human-AI co-thinking workflow that enforces reaction-network invariance.
Used network reasoning to identify pathway branch points for acetate formation in CO2 reduction.
Translated inferred levers into a prospective Cu-Fe oxide catalyst design.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports prospective synthesis and experimental confirmation of a copper-iron oxide catalyst that achieved a threefold increase in acetate selectivity versus matched Cu-rich baselines.
watch_item: Performance depends on the quality and completeness of the explicit reaction network, so missing mechanistic steps or poor graph specification could limit reliability.
paper_title: Reaction-network reasoning with frontier models for experimentally confirmed catalyst-selectivity hypotheses
